coredata.dc:title: PANI/Graphene quantum dots/graphene co-coated compressed non-woven towel for wearable energy storage
coredata.dc:creator: Shao, Feng
Hu, Nantao
Su, Yanjie
Li, Hong
Li, Bin
Zou, Cheng
Li, Gang
Yang, Zhi
Zhang, Yafei
coredata.dc:description: Nowadays, the flexible electric-power sources such as supercapacitors (SCs) have attracted increasing attention. Graphene fibers, graphene film and graphene hydrogel have been widely applied as flexible electrode materials for SCs. However, it is still a big challenge to produce large-scale graphene based flexible electrodes. In this work, we novelly coated the reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanosheets on the commercial compressed non-woven towel (CNWT), a super hydrophilic non-woven cotton fabric material, through a facile repeated “dyeing and drying” method. The rGO coated CNWT (G/CNWT) performed excellent flexibility, stability and reasonable conductivity. Purposefully, the OH-functionalized graphene quantum dots (OH-GQDs) were inserted between the graphene nanosheets and the OH-GQDs and rGO co-coated CNWT (GG/CNWT) displayed better electrochemical performance. Furthermore, homogenous polyaniline (PANI) nanoparticles were electrodeposited on the GG/CNWT (PGG/CNWT) for high areal capacitance (Ca) SCs. Finally, the electrochemical performances of G/CNWT, GG/CNWT and PGG/CNWT based electrodes were investigated and analyzed under a conventional three-electrode test. The Ca of PGG/CNWT reached to 195 m F cm−2 at 0.1 mA cm−2 and showed excellent electrochemical stabilities with a high capacity retention of 96.5 % after 6000 cycles at a current density of 5 mA cm-2. Our work showed that the super hydrophilic CNWT could be a newly flexible supporting substrate for fabricating the rGO-functionalized textile electrode. In addition, the current simple and easy to scale-up method for fabricating the PGG/CNWT electrode made it used in flexible and wearable electronics as well as other applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Graphene quantum dots
PANI
Compressed non-woven towel
Textile electrode
Flexible supercapacitors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of G/CNWT,GG/CNWT and PGG/CNWT flexible electrodes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrations of the fabrication procedure of PGG/CNWT based flexible electrode.
(a) Digital photos of CNWT(lift), GO/CNWT(middle) and G/CNWT (right). (b, c) Digital photos of dried flexible GO/CNWT and G/CNWT. (d) Making large through-holes on GG/CNWT.

SEM images of G/CNWT at low (a) and high (b) magnification. (c) The cross-sectional SEM images of the G/CNWT fiber. (d-f) Different magnification SEM images of rGO on fibers revealing the rGO stably attached on the surface of fibers.

(a) TEM image of OH-GQDs showing the morphologies and lateral sizes (inset: photograph of aqueous solution of OH-GQDs taken under UV light). (b) AFM image of OH-GQDs showing the thickness of OH-GQDs. (c) Roman spectrum of OH-GQDs. (d) XRD pattern of OH-GQDs.

(a) SEM image of GG/CNWT. (b) SEM image of GG/CNWT at high magnification. (c-d) SEM images of PGG/CNWT at different magnification showing the PANI was uniformly electrodeposited on rGO nanosheets. (e) TEM image of GO. (f) TEM image of the composite of OH-GQDs and GO revealing the OH-GQDs successfully attached onto the GO nanosheets.

The electrochemical properties comparsion of G/CNWT, GG/CNWT and PGG/CNWT: (a) CV curves at 10 mV s−1；(b) GCD curves at 0.1 mA cm-2; (c) Nyquist plots. (d) The Ca comparsion of G/CNWT and GG/CNWT. (e) GCD cuvers of GG/CNWT at different current density. (f) CV curves of PGG/CNWT at different scan rates. (g) GCD cuvers of PGG/CNWT at different current density. (h) Electrochemical stabilities of PGG/CNWT at a current density of 5 mA cm-2 for 6000 cycles.

head.ce:title: PANI/Graphene quantum dots/graphene co-coated compressed non-woven towel for wearable energy storage

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Thin Film and Microfabrication Technology (Ministry of Education), School of Electronics, Information and Electrical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Dong Chuan Road No.800, Shanghai, 200240, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Thin Film and Microfabrication Technology (Ministry of Education)
School of Electronics, Information and Electrical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Thin Film and Microfabrication Technology (Ministry of Education), School of Electronics, Information and Electrical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Dong Chuan Road No.800, Shanghai, 200240, P. R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nowadays, the flexible electric-power sources such as supercapacitors (SCs) have attracted increasing attention. Graphene fibers, graphene film and graphene hydrogel have been widely applied as flexible electrode materials for SCs. However, it is still a big challenge to produce large-scale graphene based flexible electrodes. In this work, we novelly coated the reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanosheets on the commercial compressed non-woven towel (CNWT), a super hydrophilic non-woven cotton fabric material, through a facile repeated “dyeing and drying” method. The rGO coated CNWT (G/CNWT) performed excellent flexibility, stability and reasonable conductivity. Purposefully, the OH-functionalized graphene quantum dots (OH-GQDs) were inserted between the graphene nanosheets and the OH-GQDs and rGO co-coated CNWT (GG/CNWT) displayed better electrochemical performance. Furthermore, homogenous polyaniline (PANI) nanoparticles were electrodeposited on the GG/CNWT (PGG/CNWT) for high areal capacitance (Ca) SCs. Finally, the electrochemical performances of G/CNWT, GG/CNWT and PGG/CNWT based electrodes were investigated and analyzed under a conventional three-electrode test. The Ca of PGG/CNWT reached to 195 m F cm−2 at 0.1 mA cm−2 and showed excellent electrochemical stabilities with a high capacity retention of 96.5 % after 6000 cycles at a current density of 5 mA cm-2. Our work showed that the super hydrophilic CNWT could be a newly flexible supporting substrate for fabricating the rGO-functionalized textile electrode. In addition, the current simple and easy to scale-up method for fabricating the PGG/CNWT electrode made it used in flexible and wearable electronics as well as other applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, more and more people pursue the light and portable life style. A lot of electronic equipment starts to conform to the trend and becomes more portable and wearable [1]. As one of the core of electronic equipment, the energy storage device must be more flexible to meet the demand. Supercapacitors (SCs) have many attractive advantages over batteries as an energy storage device, such as longer operation life and higher power density. Therefore, more and more attention has been paid to the electrode materials for flexible SCs [2–6]. Graphene has become the fastest growing topic in science and technology, which is associated with its strongest mechanical strength ever measured, electrical conductivity, giant intrinsic carrier mobility and record thermal conductivity [7–11]. Relatively, graphene has been widely studied as SCs electrode materials for the last few decades [12–16] because of its inspiring properties such as low weight, high specific surface area and good electrical conductivity.

Graphene as the electrode material has been made into 2D paper-like graphene films to meet the flexibility of SCs [17–19]. Huang et al. [20] fabricated a super flexible reduced graphene oxide (rGO) films through a novel methodology. The solid-state SCs made from the rGO films performed very flexible and little noticeable decrease in capacity after 2000 cycles of double-folding. Graphene oxide (GO) films are typically used as a precursor to produce rGO films and they are commonly prepared by vacuum-assisted filtration, evaporation induced self-assembly, wet-spinning of GO suspensions and electrospray coating [21–24]. However, it is still a big challenge to fabricate purely graphene film on a large scale, which hinders its practical application in flexible electronics [25–27].

To date, many textile-based electrodes were fabricated by coating electrode materials on various supporting substrates, such as cotton, polyester, carbon cloths, air-laid paper and so on [28–34]. Li et al. [35] successfully coated the graphene on the SnCl2 modified polyester by a facile and scalable repeated “dyeing and drying” strategy, which exhibited attractive electrochemical performance and good mechanical properties. It provides new research thinking that the textile-based electrode is one of the most promising electrodes for scalable development of flexible and wearable electronics.

In our work, we firstly coated the rGO on commercial compressed non-woven towel (CNWT), a super hydrophilic non-woven cotton fabric material, through a facile and scalable repeated “dyeing and drying” method. The rGO nanosheets were successfully coated on CNWT (G/CNWT) without introducing any impurity ions. When introducing high water-soluble and OH-functionalized graphene quantum dots into the dye of GO aqueous dispersion(OH-GQDs:GO = 1:10, WOH-GQDs:WGO), the CNWT was excellently coated with OH-GQDs and rGO nanosheets (GG/CNWT). The rGO nanosheets attached on fibers of GG/CNWT were more smoother and the GG/CNWT showed a reduced hydrophobicity by comparisons to the G/CNWT. Both the G/CNWT and GG/CNWT performed a high conductivity with 61 S m−1 and 50 S m−1, respectively. The G/CNWT based textile electrode displayed a high specific areal capacitance (Ca) of 128 m F cm-2 at 0.1 mA cm-2, corresponding to gravimetric capacitance (Cm) of 241 F g−1 at 0.19 A g−1 based on the mass of the coated rGO. In order to further improve the Ca, homogenous polyaniline (PANI) nanoparticles were electrodeposited on the rGO nanosheets of GG/CNWT. The electrochemical properties of PANI electrodeposited GG/CNWT (PGG/CNWT) were greatly improved. The Ca of PGG/CNWT increased by 52 percent to 195 m F cm-2 at 0.1 mA cm-2 corresponding to a high Cm of 543 F g−1 at 0.71 A g−1 based on the mass of the deposited PANI and it showed excellent electrochemical stabilities with a high capacity retention of 96.5 % after 6000 cycles at a current density of 5 mA cm-2. Our work showed that the super hydrophilic CNWT could be a newly flexible supporting substrate for fabricating the rGO-functionalized textile electrode which can be used in flexible and wearable electronics as well as other applications.

All the reagents were analytically pure without further purification and the Graphite powder (500 meshes) used in this study was purchased from Shandong Jinrilai Co., Ltd. All of the other chemical reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd.

Our previously reported modified Hummers’ method was followed to synthesize GO from graphite powder (500 meshes) [36]. Typically, concentrated sulfuric acid (100 mL) were added into a beaker (500 mL) which contained graphite powder (4.0 g) and stirred for half an hour. Then NaNO3 (2.0 g) was added and stirred for 2 h at 0 ℃. Successively, KMnO4 (14.0 g) was slowly added with stirring while keeping the temperature below 5 ℃. The mixture was then warmed to 35 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, DI water (180 mL) was added slowly with a temperature rising and stirred for another 15 min at 95 ℃. Finally, H2O2 (20 mL, 30 %) was added slowly with forming bubbles and the obtained yellow GO aqueous dispersion was washed with 1.0 M HCl solution and dialysis for one week.

The OH-GQDs in this paper are synthesized according to the reported literature [37]. Firstly, pyrene (4 g) was nitrated in hot HNO3 (320 mL) at 80 °C for 12 h and the yellow precipitate was washed with deionized water to get trinitropyrene. The trinitropyrene (900 mg) was disperised in NaOH aqueous solution (180 mL, 0.2 M) by ultrasonication for 2 h. The suspension was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. The product containing water-soluble OH-GQDs was filtered to remove insoluble carbon product.The obtained OH-GQDs was dialyzed in the dialysis bag for 2 days and 400 mg purified OH-GQDs were finally prepared after freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of G/CNWT,GG/CNWT and PGG/CNWT flexible electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: A commercial compressed non-woven towel (20 cm*24 cm) was soaked into the dye of GO aqueous dispersion (10 mg/mL, 15 mL) and then dried fully at 60 °C. These steps were repeated for three times to prepare GO coated CNWT (GO/ CNWT), the mass loading of GO on CNWT was about 0.9 mg cm−2. The G/CNWT was available by reduced GO coated CNWT with hydrazine hydrate steam at 95 °C for 5 h and the mass loading of rGO on CNWT was about 0.53 mg cm−2. The preparation method of GG/CNWT was as same as G/CNWT except adding additional OH-GQDs to GO aqueous dispersion, the mass ratio of OH-GQDs to GO is 10 % and the mass loading of OH-GQDs on CNWT is about 0.09 mg cm−2. The PGG/CNWT was fabricated via electrochemical deposition in a three-electrode system using 0.2 M aniline and 0.5 M H2SO4 aqueous solution as the electrode, a Pt foil as the counter electrode, a standard Hg/HgCl as the reference electrode and a certain area of GG/CNWT as the working electrode. The electrochemical deposition process was performed at a static potential of 0.9 V for 10 min. After the deposition, the PGG/CNWT was cleaned ultrasonically in deionied water for several times to remove the large and unstable PANI particles on the fibers. Finally, the mass loading of PANI on CNWT is about 0.14 mg cm−2. Overall, the content ratio of rGO to OH-GQDs and PANI is 0.53 : 0.09 : 0.14.

Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) images were collected on Field-Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM, Carl Zeiss Ultra 55). Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) images were collected on TEM (Talos L120C G2). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained using Bruker D8 Advance using Cu Kα radiation. The interlayer distance (d) was calculated according to the following formula: 2dsinθ=nλ, n = 1, λ = 0.15406. Atomic force microscope (AFM) images was fabricated on AFM (Nanonavi E-Sweep). The conductivity value was tested by four-point probe text and used the following equation： R □ = 1 / σ d , where R □ stands for sheet resistance (Ω sq−1), σ stands for conductivity value (S m-1), d stands for the film thickness (m).

In this work, electrochemical performances were carried out at room temperature with a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CH Instrument Company, China). The electrochemical performances of single electrode were investigated under a conventional three-electrode test. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out with a frequency ranging from 105 to 10−2 Hz. All specific capacitances of the electrode were calculated from the curves of galvanostatic charge/discharge measurements and used the following equation: Ca=(IΔt)/(SΔV), Cm=Ca/ρa where Ca stands for the areal capacitance (Ca, mF cm−2), I stands for the charge/discharge current (A), Δt is the discharge time (s), S is the area of electrode (cm2), ΔV is the potential window (V), Cm stands for the gravimetric capacitance (Cm, F g-1), ρa is the areal density (mg cm−2).

In this work, we creatively used CNWT, a super hydrophilic non-woven cotton fabric material, as the flexible supporting substrate and GO nanosheets which serve as the dyestuff were excellently absorbed on the fibers of CNWT through a facile “dipping and drying” method. The rGO sheets were still stably anchored on the fibers after reducing by hydrazine hydrate steam. Fig. 1 illustrated the fabrication procedure of PGG/CNWT based flexible textile electrode. Firstly, a commercial CNWT was dipped into GO and OH-GQDs aqueous solution which serves as the dyestuff, and the GO and OH-GQDs were quickly dyed onto CNWT due to its excellent hydrophilicity. The “dipping and drying” process was repeated for three times to enhance the mass loading of GO. The GG/CNWT was obtained after reducing the GO/CNWT by N2H4
H2O steam in a sealed bottle at 95 °C for 5 h. The PANI nanoparticles were homogenously grown on the rGO nanosheets by a facile electrodeposition method under three-electrode configuration.

The dyestuff of 15 mL deionized water containning GO nanosheets was absolutely absorbed by a piece of CNWT (20 cm*24 cm) which has been shown in Video 1. The colour of GO/CNWT and G/CNWT was yellow and silver gray (Fig. 2 a), corresponding to the colour of GO and rGO, respectively. The digital photos of dried flexible GO/CNWT and G/CNWT were shown in Figs. 2 b and c. The mass loading of rGO on CNWT was 0.53 mg cm−2 which was determined by weighing the difference of CNWT before and after coating rGO. The G/CNWT performed extremely good flexiblily and 1.12 cm2 through-holes can be arbitrarily made on it.

The CNWT was composed of the intertwined cotton fibers with a diameter of 10−15 μm and the surface of the fibers were smooth, as shown in Fig. S3. Fig. 3 was the SEM image of G/CNWT. Figs. 3 a and 3b were the SEM images of G/CNWT at low and high magnification. The figure showed the rGO sheets were uniformly and smoothly coated on the fibers. Fig. 3 c was the cross-section SEM image of the fiber, under scanning electron microscopy, the interior of the fiber displayed deep colour, but the surface was bright because the cotton fiber was non-conductive while rGO coated on the surface was conductive. Figs. 3d, 3e, and 3f were the SEM images of rGO coated fibers under different magnifications. A uncoated rGO region was found and presented deep colour. The uncoated rGO region was surrounded by coated rGO sheets, as shown in Fig. 3f, which revealed the rGO nanosheets were stably attached on the surface of fibers. The uncoated rGO region also reflected the smooth surface morphology of CNWT. In order to further prove the stability of rGO sheets on the fiber surface, the G/CNWT was put into the water under ultrasound at 40 kHz frequency. It was observed that no rGO flakes were fell off from the fibers even after 10 min of ultrasound and a small amount of rGO flakes began to fall off after 20 min of ultrasound, as shown in Fig. S4. The electrical conductivity of G/CNWT reached up to 61 S m−1.

Through the SEM images of G/CNWT, some rGO sheets are not completely coated on the surface of the fiber, but only attached to it. Here, we introduced a reported OH-functionalization of the graphene quantum dots (OH-GQDs) into the dyestuff and the fiber surface of GG/CNWT co-coated with OH-GQDs and rGO fibers encouragingly presented more smooth, as shown in Fig. 5 a and b. As shown in Fig. S5, the water contact angle of GG/CNWT decreased from 130° to 120° compared with G/CNWT, indicating that the degree of hydrophobicity of GG/CNWT was decreased which was probably caused by the high water solubility of OH-GQDs. The OH-functionalization of the graphene quantum dots presented high water solubility which has been confirmed in the above literature. In Fig. 4 ,a, the TEM image of OH-GQDs showed its uniform lateral sizes and the insert digital photo was the aqueous solution of OH-GQDs taken under UV light. Their thicknesses characterized by AFM (Fig. 4b) are largely in the range of 1 nm–3 nm. The Raman spectrum (Fig. 4c) showed the high graphitization, where the ordered G band at 1582 cm−1 was stronger than the disordered D band at 1372 cm−1. The XRD revealed a 3.34 Å layer spacing identical to that of bulk graphite (Fig. 4 d) [38]. The OH-GQDs can attached on the GO nanosheets through the π-π stacking, as shown in Fig. 5f, the OH-GQDs were uniformly anchored on GO nanosheet while pure GO nanosheet was very clean without any impurities (Fig. 5e). The electrical conductivety of GG/CNWT was slightly lower than that of G/CNWT and reached up to 50 S m−1.

PANI-based materials have been widely studied for the electrode of SCs [39]. In this work, homogenous PANI nanoparticles (about 100 nm) were electrodepositied on the rGO nanosheets. The PGG/CNWT was fabricated via eletrochemical deposition under three-electrode system in 0.2 M aniline and 0.5 M H2SO4 aqueous solution at a static potential of 0.9 V for 10 min, as shown in Fig. S6a. Figs. 5 c and 5d were the SEM images of PGG/CNWT at different magnifications which showed the PANI was uniformly and stability electrodeposited on rGO nanosheets, the mass loading of PANI is about 0.14 mg cm−2. After depositing the PANI on GG/CNWT, the colour changed to blackish green from silver gray, as shown in Fig. S6b. The PGG/CNWT still maintained high flexibility which could be bended and folded at any angle, Fig. S6c showed the state of PGG/CNWT folded three times. Fig. S7a is the Raman spectra of GO, G/CNWT, GG/CNWT and PGG/CNWT, the D (1345 cm-1)/G (1594 cm-1) peak intensity ratio of G/CNWT and GG/CNWT increased from 075 to 1.03, indicating the coated GO on CNWT was completely reduced by N2H4
H2O steam. As shown in the Raman spectra of PGG/CNWT, two new representative peaks arising from PANI can be indexed at 1488 cm-1and 1164 cm-1 corresponding to CC stretching vibration and C–H bending of the quinoid ring, which agreed well with the reported literature [40]. This result demonstrated that the PANI was successfully electrodeposited on the surface of the fibers. In the XRD pattern of G/CNWT, the rGO that was coated on the fiber of CNWT and was reduced with N2H4
H2O steam had peaks at 20.1° and 21.8°, as shown in Fig. S7b. It is known that the reduction of GO with hydrazine hydrate was accompanied by the spillage of gaseous species, such as H2O and CO2, which can restrained the rGO sheets stacking. The peak at 20.1° corresponded to an interlayer distance of 0.44 nm, revealing the existence of expanded stacking of more corrugated or disordered graphene sheets [20]. Moreover, the broad peak at 21.8° can be ascribed to the interlayer spacing of few‐layer graphene [41]. The center position of the peak at 20.8° of the XRD pattern of GG/CNWT and PGG/CNWT shifted to the left obviously, which was possibly as a result of the influence of OH-GQDs and PANI. In addition, the electrical conductivety of PGG/CNWT was intensified to 69 S m-1.

Such highly flexibe and conductive G/CNWT, GG/CNWT and PGG/CNWT based textile electrodes have shown a promising ability in SCs. The electrochemical performances of G/CNWT, GG/CNWT and PGG/CNWT based textile electrodes were investigated under a conventional three-electrode test in 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte. Figs. 6 a, 6b and 6c were the comparsion of electrochemical properties of G/CNWT, GG/CNWT and PGG/CNWT. Fig. 6 a plotted the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves at 10 mV s−1 and the CV curve of GG/CNWT was closer to the rectangular compared with G/CNWT, indicating a superior rate performance. The CV curve of PGG/CNWT displayed higher response current density and an obvious redox peaks, the larger area of the CV curve suggested the higher capacitive properties were achieved. Fig. 6 b was the galvanostatic charge-discharge curves (GCD) of G/CNWT, GG/CNWT and PGG/CNWT at 0.1 mA cm-2. The G/CNWT electrode could achieve an Ca of 128 m F cm-2 at 0.1 mA cm-2 corresponding to gravimetric capacitance (Cm) of 241 F g-1 at 0.19 A g-1 based on the mass of the coated rGO. The Ca of GG/CNWT at 0.1 mA cm-2 was slightly down to 119 m F cm-2. On the contrary, the Ca of GG/CNWT was higher than that of G/CNWT at high current density, as shown in Fig. 6 d. More specifically, the Ca of GG/CNWT reached 81 m F cm-2 at 1 mA cm-2 and 50 m F cm-2 at 5 mA cm-2 while 78 m F cm-2and 31 m F cm-2 for G/CNWT, respectively. In addition, the Ca of PGG/CNWT electrode was intensified to 195 m F cm-2 at 0.1 mA cm-2 which was about 1.6 times higher than that of GG/CNWT, the contributive Ca of the PANI in PGG/CNWT is 76 m F cm-2 corresponding to a high Cm of 543 F g-1 at 0.71 A g-1 based on the mass of the deposited PANI. Encouragingly the electrochemical performances of PGG/CNWT based electrode exceeded that of many other graphene functinalized textile electrodes reported so far (Table S1).

The Nyquist plots were displayed in Fig. 6 c and the inset was the enlarged plot in a high-frequency region where the intercept and the radius of the semicircle on real axis represented the equivalent series resistances (Rs) and the charge transfer resistance (Rct), respectively [42]. The Rs and Rct of G/CNWT, GG/CNWT and PGG/CNWT decreased in turn, which revealed that their electrochemical properties were improved in turn. In addition, the EIS profile of both GG/CNWT and PGG/CNWT was characterized by a arc in the high-frequency region followed by a nearly vertical line in the low-frequency, indicating their faster ion diffusion. Differently, the EIS profile of G/CNWT in the medium-frequency presented a longer Warburg-type line (the slope of the 45° region of the plots), which showed that G/CNWT had higher diffusion resistance [43]. Based on the above EIS profile analysis, why Ca of GG/CNWT was higher than that of G/CNWT at high current density was explained. Figs. 6 e and 6 g were the GCD curves of GG/CNWT and PGG/CNWT at different current densities, the nearly symmetrical and linear GCD curves demonstrated their good EDLC behavior. Fig. 6 f showed the CV curves of PGG/CNWT at different scan rates, the shape of CV curves of PGG/CNWT still maintained nearly rectangular at 200 mV/s, indicating a satisfactory rate performance. Besides, the PGG/CNWT electrode performed good capacitance stability with a 95.6 % capacity retention after 6000 cycles, only 0.0073 % reduction per cycle, as shown in Fig. 6 h.

In this work, a commercial compressed non-woven towel, a super hydrophilic non-woven cotton fabric material, was firstly used as the flexible supporting substrate for fabricating the rGO/OH-GQDs/PANI co-coated textile electrode for wearable SCs. The rGO nanosheets and graphene quantum dots were successfully coated on CNWT through a facile and scalable repeated “dyeing and drying” method without introducing any impurity ions. The G/CNWT and GG/CNWT performed extremely good mechanical and electrochemical properties. No rGO flakes fell off from the fibers even under ultrasound at 40 kHz frequency for 10 min. An areal capacitance of 128 m F cm−2 at 0.1 mA cm-2 was achieved by the G/CNWT electrode. The introduction of OH-functionalized graphene quantum dots between the graphene nanosheets reduced the ion diffusion resistance and the GG/CNWT displayed better electrochemical performance than G/CNWT at high current density. After electrodepositing PANI nanoparticles on the surface of rGO nanosheets, the areal capacitance of PGG/CNWT electrode was intensified to 195 m F cm-2 at 0.1 mA cm-2 corresponding to a high Cm of 543 F g-1 at 0.71 A g-1 based on the mass of the deposited PANI, which exceeded that of many other textile electrodes reported so far. Our work showed that the super hydrophilic CNWT could be a newly flexible supporting substrate for fabricating rGO coated textile electrode which can be used in flexible and wearable electronics as well as other applications.

Feng Shao: Investigation, Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Nantao Hu: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Resources. Yanjie Su: Software, Resources. Hong Li: Data curation, Methodology, Investigation. Bin Li: Formal analysis. Cheng Zou: . Gang Li: Writing - review & editing. Zhi Yang: Resources. Yafei Zhang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 61574091), Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai (17ZR1414100), and the Foundation for SMC Excellent Young Teacher in Shanghai Jiao Tong University are gratefully acknowledged. We also acknowledge analysis supports from the Instrumental Analysis Center of Shanghai Jiao Tong University and the Center for Advanced Electronic Materials and Devices of Shanghai Jiao Tong University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.synthmet.2020.116571.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in flexible–wearable solar cells for self-powered electronic devices
Energy Environ. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.A. Hashemi, S. Ramakrishna, A.G. Aberle, Recent progress in flexible–wearable solar cells for self-powered electronic devices, Energy Environ. Sci. 13 (2020) 685-743.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanocellulose-graphene composites: a promising nanomaterial for flexible supercapacitors
Carbohyd. Polym.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Xing, P. Tao, Z. Wu, C. Xing, X. Liao, S. Nie, Nanocellulose-graphene composites: a promising nanomaterial for flexible supercapacitors, Carbohyd. Polym. 207 (2019) 447–459.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Flexible electrodes and supercapacitors for wearable energy storage: a review by category
J. Mater. Chem. A

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.B. Dong, C.J. Xu, Y. Li, Z.H. Huang, F. Kang, Q.H. Yang, X. Zhao, Flexible electrodes and supercapacitors for wearable energy storage: a review by category, J. Mater. Chem. A, 4 (2016) 4659–4685.
